info.title: Keeping API Documentation
info.description:
# Introduction

Ready to create an integration with [Keeping](https://keeping.nl)? Developers can use our [RESTful](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Representational_state_transfer) API to manipulate time entries programmatically, fetch reports, manage users, projects, tasks and clients. Use the API by communicating with the accompanying web service over [HTTPS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTPS) at `api.keeping.nl/v1`. The [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON) format is used for both request and response bodies.

## Quick start

To obtain quick access to the web service, and start using the API, navigate to [**Preferences**](https://keeping.nl/account/preferences) and enable **Show features for developers.**. When activated two menu options **OAuth** and **Access tokens** will appear. There you can either generate a personal access token to try the API yourself, or configure an OAuth2 client for others to use your integration. For a quick start, choose the personal access token.

Once you have generated a personal access token, use it to send a `GET` request to: [`https://api.keeping.nl/v1/organisations`](#tag/organisations/paths/~1organisations/get) with an `Authorization` header containing `Bearer {your personal access token}`. For example, you could use [cURL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CURL) in the command line as follows:

```bash
curl -XGET https://api.keeping.nl/v1/organisations \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer {your access token}" \
     -H "Accept: application/json"
```

This command will return a JSON object containing all the organisations you have access to. Select one to use and send subsequent requests to: [`https://api.keeping.nl/v1/{organisation_id}/{…}`](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get) in order to access and manipulate data within your Keeping organisation. For example, use:

```bash
curl -XGET https://api.keeping.nl/v1/{organisation_id}/users/me
     -H "Authorization: Bearer {your access token}" \
     -H "Accept: application/json"
```

to get information about your user within the selected organisation. Nice 👍, you are off to a great start. Now read 📖 along to discover how to use the Keeping API to the fullest.

# Authentication

To start using the Keeping API, you will need to have an [account](https://keeping.nl/register) with Keeping, be part of an organisation within Keeping and have an access token. You can either be part of an organisation as an administrator or just a regular team member. Team members have **limited access**, and can only use a subset of the API.

You can obtain an access token in one of two ways: use a [personal access token](#section/Authentication/Personal-access-tokens) directly linked to your Keeping account,
or implement an [OAuth2 authentication flow](#section/Authentication/OAuth2) to allow other Keeping customers to use your integration.

Our OAuth2 implementation follows [RFC 6749](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749).

## Personal access tokens

If you want to experiment with our API or want to write a one-off integration for yourself, you can use a **personal access token**. To acquire a personal access token:

1. [Login to Keeping](https://keeping.nl/auth/login).
2. Navigate to **My account** using the dropdown in the upper right corner, under your avatar.
3. Navigate to **For developers** in the vertical menu on the left.
4. Click on the green **Create access token** button next to the header: **Personal access tokens**.
5. Select the appropriate [scopes](#section/Authentication/Scopes) you wish to use if you are not sure you can select all of them for an experimental setup.
6. The new access token is displayed, you should copy it from the presented screen as it is only shown once.

Make sure your access token functions correctly by sending the following request, using cURL or another tool.

```bash
curl -XGET https://api.keeping.nl/v1/organisations \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer {your access token}" \
     -H "Accept: application/json"
```

Personal access tokens are valid for one year. You can revoke them yourself from the **Developer** screen within Keeping.

> **Please note:** be very careful with your personal access token, somebody with access to it can quickly get access to all your data within Keeping. Do not share a personal access token with other people or paste it into external applications. External applications should use the [OAuth2](#section/Authentication/OAuth2) authentication flow.

Both personal access tokens as well as regular OAuth2 access tokens conform to the [JWT format](https://jwt.io/) and can be verified using our [public key](https://developer.keeping.nl/oauth_jwt_public.key). Access tokens should be sent along in every request after authentication. You should add them as bearer tokens in the `Authorization` HTTP header prefixed with the string `Bearer `.

## OAuth2

Use the OAuth2 authentication flow if you want to allow other Keeping customers to use the integration you are developing. Before you start authenticating a user, you should register a new OAuth2 application within Keeping yourself. To do this:

1. [Login to Keeping](https://keeping.nl/auth/login).
2. Navigate to **My account** using the dropdown in the upper right corner, under your avatar.
3. Navigate to **For developers** in the vertical menu on the left.
4. Click on the green **Register application** button next to the header: **Your applications (OAuth clients)**.
5. Enter a name that describes your application clearly. This name will be displayed to users when Keeping asks them whether they grant you access to their data.
6. Enter a redirect URL that Keeping can use to direct users to your application after they accept to share their Keeping data with you. The redirect URL could be something like: `https://ella-evenementen.nl/keeping-redirect/` or use a custom scheme like `myapp://keeping-redirect`.
7. A new OAuth client is created, and its `client_id` and `client_secret` are shown in the table.

> **Please note:** Make sure never to share the `client_secret` with other parties. When there is any indication that your `client_secret` could be compromised, you should delete the current OAuth2 client in Keeping and create a new one. Afterwards, you should swap the `client_id` and `client_secret` in your code, and distribute a new version of your software if necessary.

### The OAuth2 authentication flow

To start the authentication process using OAuth2, you should direct your users to `https://keeping.nl/oauth/authorize?…` including some of the URL parameters specified below. The resulting page will ask the user to login and, once logged in, ask the user to grant your application access to their data. When a user accepts, Keeping will send them back with an **authorisation code** to the redirect URL you provided while setting up the OAuth2 client. You can use this authorization code to request an access token.

If you are not operating within the context of the web, like when you have a native desktop or mobile app, you should use a webview library or external browser for opening this URL. The redirect URL supports custom schemes, i.e. your application could provide something like `myapp://keeping-redirect` as redirect URL.

**Start the process by directing your user to the URL:**

```
https://keeping.nl/oauth/authorize?client_id={your_apps_oauth_client_id}&redirect_uri={your_apps_redirect_uri_urlencoded}&response_type=code&scope=time+team+reporting+project_management&state={your_optional_nonce}
```

You can provide the following URL parameters:

| URL parameter for authorisation |               |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------- | ------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `client_id`                     | (required)    | The `client_id` for the OAuth2 client you registered as a developer with Keeping.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| `response_type`                 | (required)    | Should have the value `code`, other response types are not supported.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| `scope`                         | (required)    | Provide the [scopes](#section/Authentication/Scopes) you wish to use. When using multiple, chain them using the `+` character, e.g. `time+team`. _Please note: do not use `%2B` to chain scopes._                                                                                                                    |
| `redirect_uri`                  | (recommended) | Provide the exact redirect URL you setup within Keeping in [url encoded format](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Percent-encoding). So if you have entered a redirect URL like: `https://ella-evenementen.nl/keeping-redirect/` you should provide the value `https%3A%2F%2Fella-evenementen.nl%2Fkeeping-redirect%2F`. |
| `state`                         | (optional)    | A value that will be sent along when Keeping redirects the user back to your application. You can use this to identify the result.                                                                                                                                                                                   |

> **Please note:** Do not capture a user's login information or fill the Keeping login form programmatically. This is not a safe method for interacting with an OAuth2 authentication flow.
> If we find that your application is capturing, storing or programmatically handling a user's Keeping credentials, we will disable access for your OAuth2 clients.

The authorisation screen the user will see, after login, looks something like this:

![Example OAuth2 authorisation screen](https://keeping.nl/images/developer/oauth-screen.png 'Example OAuth2 authorisation screen')

If something goes wrong, or the user denies the request, the user will be sent back to your application using the redirect URL you provided.
To let you know what went wrong Keeping will send along a couple of URL parameters:

| URL parameters for redirect on failure |                                                                                                                                                                        |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `error`                                | Right now could be: `access_denied`, `invalid_request`, `unauthorized_client`, `unsupported_response_type`, `invalid_scope`, `server_error`, `temporarily_unavailable` |
| `error_description`                    | Optional English description of the error. For now, this description is **not** localised according to the `Accept-Language` header, but might be in the future.       |
| `hint`                                 | Optional English hint on how to solve the error. For now, this hint is **not** localised according to the `Accept-Language` header, but might be in the future.        |
| `state`                                | The exact same value you provided when directing the user to Keeping's authorize URL.                                                                                  |

When the user clicks on **Accept** they will be sent back to your application using the redirect URL you provided, along with the **authorisation code** in the URL parameter `code`, e.g. `https://ella-evenementen.nl/keeping-redirect/?code={authorisation_code}`.

| URL parameters for redirect on success |                                                                                       |
| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `code`                                 | The authorisation code you can use to request an access token.                        |
| `state`                                | The exact same value you provided when directing the user to Keeping's authorize URL. |

The authorisation code is valid for **60 minutes** after it has been issued, so make sure you request an access token immediately after receiving the authorisation code.

### Request an access token

Now you can request the access token. Send your authorisation code and the OAuth client details in a `POST` request to `https://api.keeping.nl/v1/oauth/token` to receive an access token.
This request requires you to send along the `grant_type`, `code`, `client_id`, `client_secret` and the `redirect_uri`. The `code` is the just received authorisation code.
The `grant_type`, in this case, should have the value `authorization_code`.
Keeping does **not** support other grant types for requesting an initial access token at the moment.
The `client_id`, `client_secret` and `redirect_uri` should correspond precisely with the details you provided to Keeping when registering your application.
Make sure to package all the required parameters as form data in the request body. Read the [documentation of the actual request](#tag/oauth/paths/~1oauth~1token/post) for more information.

You could acquire an access token with cURL as follows:

```bash
curl -XPOST https://api.keeping.nl/v1/oauth/token \
    -H "Accept: application/json" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    -d "grant_type=authorization_code" \
    -d "code={the_just_received_authorisation_code}" \
    -d "client_id={your_apps_oauth_client_id}" \
    -d "client_secret={your_apps_oauth_client_secret}" \
    -d "redirect_uri={your_apps_oauth_redirect_url}"
```

If the token is requested successfully you will receive a `200` response containing both an access token and a refresh token.

```json
{
    "token_type": "Bearer",
    "expires_in": 86400,
    "access_token": "{your_access_token}",
    "refresh_token": "{your_refresh_token}"
}
```

You can now use the value of the `access_token` property to send requests to the Keeping web service using the `Authorization` header.
Make sure to set its value to: `Bearer {your_access_token}`.
Keeping will always return a `Bearer` token. In order to comply with the OAuth2 specification,
Keeping makes sure to inform you about the type of token using the `token_type` response property.

Both personal access tokens as well as regular OAuth2 access tokens conform to the [JWT format](https://jwt.io/) and can be verified using our [public key](https://developer.keeping.nl/oauth_jwt_public.key).

OAuth2 access tokens are usually valid for **24 hours**, but this is subject to change. The `expires_in` will provide the number of seconds the access token is valid.
Make sure to calculate the exact moment of expiration yourself to prevent superfluous requests to the web service.
When an access token has expired, the server will respond with a `401` HTTP status code.
You can request a new access token using the `refresh_token`, once it has expired.

### Refresh when an access token has expired

When an access token has expired, you can request a new access token without requiring the user to follow the authentication flow again.
You can use the `refresh_token` you received alongside your original access token.
Refresh tokens are valid for **90 days** from the moment they are issued, so make sure to use it in that time to keep your session alive.

You can use the [request access token](#tag/oauth/paths/~1oauth~1token/post) request similar to when you used it to acquiring the original access token to get a new one. An example using cURL:

```bash
curl -XPOST https://api.keeping.nl/v1/oauth/token \
    -H "Accept: application/json" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    -d "grant_type=refresh_token" \
    -d "refresh_token={your_last_refresh_token}" \
    -d "client_id={your_apps_oauth_client_id}" \
    -d "client_secret={your_apps_oauth_client_secret}" \
    -d "redirect_uri={your_apps_oauth_redirect_url}" \
    -d "scope=time+team+reporting+project_management"
```

You will receive a new access token and a new refresh token, in the exact same way you received your original access token.
You can only use a refresh token once to get a new token.

## Scopes

Access to Keeping can be limited by using not all but only one, or several scopes while requesting an access token.
You could do this to mitigate security risks and inform the users what specific data and functionality your integration uses.

| Scope                | Description                                                                                                                                                     |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `time`               | Allows you to manipulate time entries for yourself, and if you combine `time` with `team` you can manipulate time entries for other users as well.              |
| `reporting`          | Allows you to use reporting functions for your time data. If you combine `reporting` with `team`, this will allow you to access reporting for your entire team. |
| `team`               | Allows you to request information about all users within the organisation if you are an administrator.                                                          |
| `project_management` | Allows you to manage projects, clients and tasks if you are an administrator.                                                                                   |

When requesting access for multiple scopes, you can chain them separated by a plus, e.g. `team+project_management`.

If you try to access a part of the API that is associated with a scope you did not specify when requesting your access token, you will receive a `403` [response](#section/General-usage/Responses) from the server.

## Authorisation

Aside from the scopes used by your personal access token or the OAuth2 client,
Keeping also checks a user's authorisation for each specific action based on their current role,
the organisations active subscription plan and specific organisation settings.
If one of these checks fails, you receive a `403` [response](#section/General-usage/Responses) from the server,
and this means you do not have the correct authorisation for the action you are trying to execute.
`401` [responses](#section/General-usage/Responses) are reserved for notifying you about invalid,
revoked or expired access tokens. Keeping checks your access token's validity before it checks your authorisation.

### The user being a member of the organisation

If you are not a member of, or are blocked from, the organisation you try to request data of, you will receive a `403` response for all requests regarding that organisation.
When an administrator archives your user, your access to that organisation is blocked. Make sure to check all your active organisations using [`GET /organisations`](#tag/organisations/paths/~1organisations/get).

### The user's role

Users within an organisation have a `role` which is a property available on the `user` object. This role is either `administrator` or `team_member`.
Only administrators are allowed to access the scopes `team` and `project_management`.
And only administrators are entitled to request reports for the entire team or manipulate the time entries of other users within the organisation.
Make sure to check your role under using [`GET /{organisation_id}/users/me`](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).

### The organisation's active plan

If your organisation's current subscription plan does not allow a specific feature to be used you will receive a `403` response as well.
Our detailed [pricing table](https://keeping.nl/prijzen/vergelijken) shows all the features for each specific plan. At the moment Keeping organisations can have one of the following subscription plans:

| Plan                                              | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `free_2024`                                       | This [free plan](https://keeping.nl/prijzen/vergelijken) allows limited Keeping features to be used. It is meant for organisations with only a single user. All requests that require the `team` scope are unavailable for an organisation using this plan. |
| Legacy: `free_2019`, `free_2018`, `standard_2018` | These plans have the same restrictions as the `free_2024` plan. With the exception of multiple users in an organisation. Organisation's with these plans are allowed to use the requests under the `team` scope.                                            |
| `plus_2024`                                       | This is our current Plus paid plan and does not have restrictions, except for features which are part of the Pro plan.                                                                                                                                      |
| `pro_2024`                                        | This is our current Pro paid plan and does not have restrictions.                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| `enterprise_2019`                                 | This is our enterprise plan which does not have any restrictions whatsoever. Enterprise features are not reflected in this API documentation as of yet.                                                                                                     |

Make sure to check your organisation's plan using [`GET /organisations`](#tag/organisations/paths/~1organisations/get).

### The organisation's feature toggles

You will also receive a `403` response if you try to use a feature which has been disabled by the organisation's administrators.
Keeping currently has the following feature toggles available on the `organisation` object:

```json
{
  …
  "features": {
    "timesheet": "hours",
    "projects": true,
    "tasks": true,
    "breaks": true
  },
  …
}
```

If, for example, **projects** are disabled you will not be able to request or modify projects. On the other hand, you will not be required to specify a project when creating time entries as well.
The same is true for **tasks**. When **breaks** are disabled you will not be able to create new or modify existing time entries with the purpose `break`.
So, make sure to check your organisation's currently enabled features using [`GET /organisations`](#tag/organisations/paths/~1organisations/get) before using any of them.

# General usage

Now that you understand our authentication mechanisms, we will explain some API characteristics that are not specific to any request but are used throughout the entire API.
Please make sure to read this chapter carefully.

## Usage etiquette

We provide the Keeping API to both our paid and free customers.
If you create an awesome integration for your software product [let us know](https://keeping.nl/contact)😄!
And if you use the API to improve your internal reporting make sure to share your experience with us as well.

If you register an OAuth2 application, make sure Keeping's customers are well informed about the types of data they share with your application.
Even though Keeping informs the user in the OAuth2 authorisation screen, we still like you to take extra care in informing them well.
Please note that administrators have access to the data of all users within the organisation.
Additionally, make sure to ask users explicitly for their permission when you want to store their Keeping data in a permanent fashion yourself.
Please be transparent about your data and privacy policy, and make sure to honour their rights under the [GDPR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation).

The Keeping API does have [usage limitations](#section/General-usage/Usage-limitations) but aside from those, we also ask you not to abuse the web service.
We reserve the right to block anyone from using the web service or to temporally disable it altogether.
Please do not send an excessive amount of requests to the web service. Do not send more than `3` requests in parallel.
Do not poll the web service periodically to check for changes—and if you absolutely need to, make sure it is once per day, during the night ([CET](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_European_Time)).
Do not call the web service as part of an automated testing procedure. Make sure to cache responses where appropriate.

Please send along with the name of your software, its exact version and the developer's e-mail address in the `User-Agent` header of the requests.

## Requests

To send a request to the Keeping web service you can use any HTTP command-line tool such as [cURL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CURL), or any general-purpose HTTP library.
We have a couple of suggestions:

- PHP: [Guzzle](http://docs.guzzlephp.org/en/stable/), [Httpful](https://github.com/nategood/httpful), [cURL](https://www.php.net/manual/en/book.cURL.php) or [file_get_contents()](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.file-get-contents.php)
- JavaScript: [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios), [superagent](https://github.com/visionmedia/superagent), [XMLHttpRequest](https://developer.mozilla.org/nl/docs/Web/API/XMLHttpRequest) or [fetch()](https://developer.mozilla.org/nl/docs/Web/API/Fetch_API)
- Python: [Requests](https://2.python-requests.org//en/latest/index.html), [httplib2](https://github.com/httplib2/httplib2) or [http.client](https://docs.python.org/3/library/http.client.html)
- Ruby: [REST Client](https://github.com/rest-client/rest-client) or [Active Resource](https://github.com/rails/activeresource)
- Swift: [Alamofire](https://github.com/Alamofire/Alamofire) (or [AFNetworking](https://github.com/AFNetworking/AFNetworking) for Objective-C)
- Java/Kotlin: [OkHttp](https://square.github.io/okhttp/)

### Request base URL

All the URL path's for the API are constructed as follows:

```
https://api.keeping.nl/{version}/{path}
```

Where `{version}` is the API version, right now only `v1` is available and is described in this document.
The `{path}` should be swapped with the specific path of the API you wish to use.
Most paths start with `{organisation_id}/` for the relevant Keeping organisation.

### Request methods

Requests made to the Keeping web service can be made using one of the following four HTTP methods.

| Method | Description                                                                                                     |
| ------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| GET    | These requests are used to fetch lists of data instances or a single data instance.                             |
| POST   | These requests are used to create new data instances.                                                           |
| PATCH  | These requests are used to mutate data instances by either updating properties or executing a mutative command. |
| DELETE | These requests are used to permanently delete data instances.                                                   |

The Keeping API does **not** support `PUT`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS` or other unspecified HTTP methods.

### Request headers

Requests sent to the web service should contain at least two headers: `Authorization` and `Accept`.
Some requests also require you to send the `Content-Type` header. The API supports the following HTTP request headers:

| Header          |                                             | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| --------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Authorization   | **required**                                | This header should contain the access token in the form of: `Bearer {your access token}`. Requests used for the authentication flow are the exception and do not require the `Authorization` header.                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Accept          | **required**                                | Describes the requested data format of the response body. This header should always have `application/json` as its value.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| Content-Type    | **required** if the request contains a body | Describes the data format of the request body. This header should have the value `application/json` when you are submitting a JSON body with a `POST` or `PATCH` request. Only the [OAuth requests](#tag/oauth/paths/~1oauth~1token/post) use `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`.                                                                                                                   |
| Accept-Language | _optional_, default: `en`                   | This header can be used to specify the preferred response language for strings containing natural language. Keeping currently supports: `nl` and `en`. When not provided, Keeping assumes `en` as this is generally the language developers work with while programming. Keeping does **not** support constructed `Accept-Language` header values with priorities and multiple languages or locales. |
| User-Agent      | _optional_                                  | Used to provide Keeping with information about your application, its version and your contact details.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

### Request parameters and body

Some `GET` requests will allow you to specify additional parameters.
You should pass these along in the URL as query parameters, for example by appending `?first=value1&second=value2` to the URL.

Some `POST` and `PATCH` requests require you send information in the form of a JSON request body.
Under 'Response body' you will find the specific JSON structure for each request. Usually, not all properties are required, and thus you are free to leave out optional properties from the root JSON object, or nested objects.
Make sure the data is encoded in the type specified in the API, i.e. when a string is expected make sure to send a string, when an integer is expected make sure to send an integer.
When packaging your data in the request body make sure to also include the `Content-Type: application/json` header in the request.
The API does **not** support other content type for encoding the request body, except for the [OAuth requests](#tag/oauth/paths/~1oauth~1token/post) which uses `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`.

## Responses

Web service responses start with an HTTP status code. Before doing anything with the response body, first, check this code to determine roughly what happened.
The Keeping web service can return responses with one of the following HTTP status codes:

| Code | Meaning               | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ---- | --------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 200  | OK                    | You have successfully received data from the web service through a `GET` request or successfully executed a mutation through a `PATCH` request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| 201  | Created               | You have successfully created new data through a `POST` request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| 204  | No content            | You have successfully deleted something through a `DELETE` request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| 400  | Bad request           | Keeping is unable to process the request because the request itself or its body contents is invalid. This could happen when the request body does not contain valid JSON, is incomplete, or has the wrong JSON structure.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| 401  | Unauthorized          | This status code will be used if the token sent along in the `Authentication` header is invalid, has been revoked or has expired. When you receive a 401 you should either request a new token with the refresh token or reauthenticate.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| 403  | Forbidden             | You are not allowed to use this part of the API, or are not allowed to use a specific instance specified in a URL parameter or request body. You could also not have enough privileges within the selected organisation, e.g. because you are not an admin but just a team member, or because the used OAuth2 client does not provide access to the scope required. Your IP address could have been blocked from using the web service as well, e.g. by systematically ignoring the [usage limitations](#section/General-usage/Usage-limitations). |
| 404  | Not found             | You are trying to access or reference data that does not exist (anymore). This could also concern a specific instance specified in a URL parameter or the request body. When previously you received data of a specific instance on the same API path, you can safely assume that specific instance has been deleted.                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| 405  | Method not allowed    | You sent a request to a URL path with a method which is not supported for that specific path, e.g. you sent a `POST` request to a path that only supports the `GET` method.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| 422  | Unprocessable entity  | Validation of a URL parameter or data in the request body has failed. Please check the response body for more information.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| 429  | Too many requests     | You have exceeded the [usage limitations](#section/General-usage/Usage-limitations). Make sure to stop calling the web service entirely for at least the amount of seconds specified in the `Retry-After` response header.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| 500  | Internal server error | Something went wrong within Keeping with your specific request. We get automatically notified of these errors, but please feel free to reach out to [support@keeping.nl](mailto:support@keeping.nl) for further information.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| 502  | Bad gateway           | There is no server available to handle your request. This could happen when Keeping is experiencing unexpected high load. Please check [status.keeping.nl](https://status.keeping.nl) for more information.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| 503  | Service unavailable   | Keeping web service has been taken offline. Sometimes this can happen during a deploy for a couple of seconds. This could also be because of planned maintenance. Please check [status.keeping.nl](https://status.keeping.nl) for more information.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| 504  | Gateway timeout       | The server handling you request was taking too long to handle it. This could happen when Keeping is experiencing unexpected high load. Please check [status.keeping.nl](https://status.keeping.nl) for more information.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |

### Response body

When a response contains a JSON body, the response header `Content-Type: application/json` is present. In general, responses with a `2xx` status code are accompanied by a JSON body with the following structure:

```json
{
  "{instance_name}" : { // Contains either a single object or an array of objects
    …
  },
  "meta" : { // Optional additional information
    …
  }
}
```

The object or array under the `{instance_name}` keypath contains the main requested, mutated or created data. For example, when requesting time entries this will be `time-entries`.
In response to a successful `DELETE` request, the value of `{instance_name}` will be `null`.
The information present in the optional object under `meta` keypath contains other useful information, e.g. regarding pagination.
If applicable, Keeping will present strings in the natural language requested using the `Accept-Language` request header.

Responses with a `4xx` or `5xx` **can** be accompanied by a JSON body with the following structure:

```json
{
  "error" : {
    "message" : "Oops… something went wrong.",
    … // Additional information could be given,
      // e.g. in the case of a 422: Unprocessable entity
  }
}
```

Please do not assume the existence of an accompanied JSON encoded error message, it could very well be that our load balancer returns an HTML document or something else entirely.
So when handling error responses, first check the status code and only if present look at the JSON encoded error message for more information.
Keeping will try to present the error message in the language requested using the `Accept-Language` request header.
Data from the error message is not suitable for use in error handling logic other than displaying a message to the user, and should always be considered subject to change.

## Pagination

In some cases, the API returns a **paginated** array of instances, for example with `GET …/{organisation_id}/projects`.
The array has a default length of `75` instances per request. To request the next `75` instances you will need to make an additional request for the next _page_.
You do this by adding the `?page=2` URL parameter. Pages are indexed starting with one, i.e. the first page is `page=1`. So, `page=0` does not exist.
The root JSON object returned by these paginated requests contains information regarding pagination under the `meta` keypath.

```json
{
  "{instance_name}": [
    …
  ],
  "meta": {
    "total": 115,
    "per_page": 25,
    "current_page": 1,
    "last_page": 5,
    …
  }
}
```

You can also add the `per_page` URL parameter to modify the number of instances returned by Keeping. The maximum value is `150`.

## Dates and time zones

Dates will be returned in the [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) date format.
Where a property is representing a specific moment in **time** the value will return a string including seconds and time zone adjustment, e.g.: `2019-11-27T20:45:10+02:00`.
This corresponds to the PHP date format: `Y-m-d\TH:i:sP`.
Where a property is representing just a specific **day**, irrespective of the exact moment on that day or the timezone,
Keeping will return/require a string in the PHP date format: `Y-m-d`, e.g. `2019-11-27`.

Time zones in Keeping are set per organisation. You can [change the time zone](https://keeping.nl/support/instellingen/tijdzone-aanpassen) in the organisation settings.
The `organisation` object does contain a `time_zone` property, the string value corresponds with the [tz database name](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database) for the selected time zone.
Times outside of the scope of an organisation will be returned in [Coordinated Universal Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time), i.e. `UTC`.
For now, the web service has **no possibility** of returning dates in a representation with another time zone than set for the organisation, neither by setting a user-specific time zone, nor by sending the web service a special time zone header.

## Server time

The current time on the Keeping servers is synced using [NTP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_Time_Protocol) to make sure it is accurate.
Keeping uses the NTP pool at: `ntp.ubuntu.com`.
To make sure you know the server time at the moment of processing your request Keeping sends along a special header in the response: `X-Server-Time-Ms`.
The value is the current [Unix Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time) on the server expressed in **milliseconds**.
Use this time to calculate the offset between your machines current time and the Keeping server time to correctly calculate hours for running time entries.
When calculating the offset between your local machine time and the server time make sure to account for the average time requests are in transit.

## Usage limitations

Aside from asking to conform to the [usage etiquette](#section/General-usage/Usage-etiquette), the Keeping API also throttles requests using a rate limiter to prevent abuse.
You can make `120` requests to the API every `1` minute for each individual Keeping account that is either associated with the used personal access token or the OAuth2 session, i.e. having multiple personal access tokens or OAuth2 sessions does not increase your rate limit.

All web service responses will contain the header `X-RateLimit-Limit` with the value `120` for the limit.
Responses also contain the header `X-RateLimit-Remaining` with the number of remaining requests, e.g. `119` after one request.

Once you have hit the rate limit, and you receive a `429`, the provided response header `Retry-After` specifies the number of seconds you should wait.
Its value will be `60`. You will also receive the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header, specifying the [Unix time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time) in seconds when you are allowed to retry.
When that time passed, the number of requests remaining will have been reset to `120`. These values are subject to change, so make sure to implement the handling of hitting the rate limiter using the actual header values.

## Miscellaneous

This API does **not** feature [Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)](https://www.w3.org/TR/cors/).

We use a lot of [open-source software at Keeping](https://keeping.nl/en/open-source-attribution).
One such piece of software is [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc) which was used to generate our [documentation website](https://developer.keeping.nl) from our [OpenAPI 3.0 specification](https://developer.keeping.nl/openapi.json).

externalDocs.description: Find more information in our knowledge base

tags.name: oauth
tags.description:
In this section, you will find documentation for requesting authentication information. Please read [this section](#section/Authentication/OAuth2) for more information on the OAuth2 authentication flow and the acquiring of an access token. Our implementation follows [RFC 6749](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749).


| Types of tokens/codes | Valid for | Remark |
|---|---|---|
| Authorization code | 60 minutes | Can only be used once. |
| Access token | 24 hours | The `expires_in` will provide the number of seconds the access token is valid. |
| Refresh token | 90 days | Can only be used once. You'll receive a new refresh token when using it. |

tags.name: organisations
tags.description:
When you use Keeping, you always do so in the context of a specific organisation. You can see one of Keeping's organisations as a workspace in which everything is contained, and usage takes place. Data from one organisation (e.g. time entries or projects) cannot be accessed or used in another organisation.    

Your Keeping account can be associated with multiple organisations, and so you yourself can be a part of multiple. When you start using Keeping, before you do anything else, you should choose which organisation you want to use. Within each organisation you have your own `user` object which can be accessed through: [`GET /{organisation_id}/users/me`](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).

Therefore, before you start using the API, you should select the organisation you want to use. The paths of all further requests are prefixed with `{organisation_id}`, in which you should specify the selected organisation. To get an overview of your options, you can [fetch all your currently active organisations](#tag/organisations/paths/~1organisations/get).

There could also be some inactive organisations. These are disabled organisations that are planned for deletion, or organisations to which your access has been blocked. You are denied access when an administrator archives your user. The API does not provide the possibility to fetch inactive organisations.

tags.name: entries
tags.description:
Use the calls below to manipulate time entries in Keeping. APIs described in this section correspond roughly with the actions you can yourself take when interacting with Keeping's timesheet, either through the app or through the website.

To use these, make sure your session has access to the `time` scope. Regular team members can only access their own time entries, in that case, you should use your own `user_id`, which you can retrieve through [`GET /{organisation_id}/users/me`](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get). If you are an administrator **and** have access to the `team` scope, you can also fetch and manipulate other user's time entries.

tags.name: reports
tags.description:
Use these APIs to retrieve reporting data from Keeping. If you want to use the hours tracked in another application, but you wish to track hours in Keeping, you should use these APIs instead of the time APIs.

Make sure you have access to the `reporting` scope. If your user is an `administrator`, you have access to all-time reports for the entire organisation. When your user is a `team_member` you'll only have access to reports on your own time entries. When you have this limited role, make sure always to pass the URL query parameter: `?user_ids[]={your_user_id}` and no additional user id, otherwise the web service will respond with a `403`.
 
There are just two URL paths used for reporting. That may seem little, however, these are both flexible and powerful due to the vast number of possible parameter combinations. You can either get an [overview](#tag/reports/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1report/get) of the hours in a specific period for a particular predicate or get the [underlying time entries](#tag/reports/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1report~1time-entries/get).

tags.name: users
tags.description:
When you have access to the `team` scope and you are an `administrator` you can retrieve the information of your entire team. However, you can always access the information of [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get), even without the `team` scope.

It is perhaps important to realise that a single Keeping account, represented by a set of login credentials, can be associated with **multiple** users entities—one per organisation. So a user within the context of this API should be considered: "a user of an organisation's Keeping environment". This means that, just as other entities, users are scoped to the selected organisation. Therefore you can have multiple "me" users for your current API session, a different one per organisation.

tags.name: projects
tags.description:
Fetch and manage your projects when `projects` are enabled.

You can always fetch the projects you can use for creating your own time entries.

You can create, edit and manage projects only when your user is an `administrator` and the session has access to the `project_management` scope.

Projects are directly linked to clients, when you have enabled `projects` you automatically have access to clients as well, and similarly when `projects` are disabled clients are as well.

<!--- just to make sure, is it intended to use the term 'participations' here instead of 'participants'? I know that this is the database term but participations seems like a weird term to describe the users. -->
The ability to create time entries for specific projects by certain team members can be restricted by the usage of `participations`, which indicate who can create time entries for that project. And if both `tasks` and `projects` are enabled for your organisation, you have the ability to limit the usage of specific tasks to certain projects through the usage or `task_assignments`. Thus, a project's `participations` tell you which users can create time entries with it, and a project's `task_assignments` tell you which tasks can be used for time entries in combination with the project.

A created project starts out as an `active` project, but can be changed to `archived` when you [archive](#tag/projects/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1projects~1{project_id}~1archive/patch) it. You can only [delete projects](#tag/projects/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1projects~1{project_id}/delete) that do **not** have any time entries associated with them.

tags.name: tasks
tags.description:
Fetch and manage your tasks when `tasks` are enabled.

You can always fetch all tasks. However, you can only create, edit and manage tasks when your user is an `administrator` and the session has access to the `project_management` scope.

You can use tasks to classify time entries to specific types of tasks or activities. For example you could have a task for: Recruitment, HR, Administration, Acquisition, Project Management, Marketing, etc. So the concept of a task in Keeping does not necessarily relate to an individual to-do but rather a classification for the type of to-do.

When you use tasks in conjunction with `projects`, you can specify per project which tasks can be performed while working on it. You do this using the `task_assignments` property on the `project` object. In this case you can only create time entries using an active task assigned to the selected project.

If you do not use `projects` you can create time entries using all active tasks. A created task starts out as an `active` task, but can be changed to `archived` when you [archive](#tag/tasks/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1tasks~1{task_id}~1archive/patch) it. You can only [delete a task](#tag/tasks/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1tasks~1{task_id}/delete) that does **not** have any time entries associated with it.

tags.name: clients
tags.description:
Manage your clients when `projects` are enabled for your organisation, your user is an `administrator` and the session has access to the `project_management` scope. 

Clients are used within Keeping primarily to group projects within the interface and for the reports. You cannot directly create time entries for a client, this has to be done through it's projects. So, clients can have projects linked to them. Therefor when you have enabled `projects` you automatically have access to clients as well, and similarly when `projects` are disabled clients are as well.

A created client starts out as an `active` client, but is changed to `inactive` when it only has archived projects associated with it. You can only [delete a client](#tag/clients/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1clients~1{client_id}/delete) that does **not** have any projects associated with it.

tags.name: tags
tags.description:
Fetch and manage your tags when `tags` are enabled.

You can always fetch all tags. Create, edit and manage is not yet possible.

You can use tags to annotate your records in more detail.

Tags can be set up in two ways: "default tags" and "linked tags".

- **Default tags**: Set which labels are filled in by default per project and/or task when registering hours. All labels within the list can be selected when registering hours.
- **Linked tags**: Set which labels can be selected per client/project and/or task when registering hours. Only the linked tags can be selected when registering hours.

get.description:
Lists all your currently active organisations. Select one to proceed using the API. 

 The `organisation` objects you receive in the response contains important information on the specific features that are available to them. Make sure you check them to ensure the correct usage of the API. You can read more details about the organisation's features in the [authorisation](#section/Authentication/Authorisation) section.

The response is **not** paginated.

organisations.description: A list of all the active organisations where you (the authenticated user) are currently a part of.

get.description:
List all users for an organisation.
In order to do this your OAuth session should have access to the `team` scope, and your own user should be an `administrator`.

This result is **paginated**, so you will need to send the server multiple requests if you want to
retrieve all users when there are more than `100`.

parameters.name: project_id
parameters.description:
Can only be used if `projects` are enabled. Filter the users down to the ones that are participating in this specific project.
When not provided Keeping will return the users for all projects, including the ones that are not participating at all.

200.description: Successfully retrieved a list of users

403.description: You are not allowed access to these users

get.description:
Fetch the organisation's user for the current OAuth session or the currently used personal access token.

You have a different "me" user entity for each organisation you have access to. Therefore, your OAuth session or personal access token can have access to **multiple** "me" users entities. 
For each organisation you should use a different `user_id` to describe your user in that organisation's context. 

Fetching your own user's data can be done even if the OAuth session does not have access to the `team` scope.

403.description: You are not allowed access to your user

get.description:
Fetch a single user's data from the organisation. Use this method to fetch a user other than your own user. 
In order to do so your OAuth session should have access to the `team` scope, and your own user should be an `administrator`.

403.description: You are not allowed to access this user

get.description:
Fetch all time entries for a single user on a specific day. The response is **not** paginated.

Do **not** use this to search through many entries.
You can use '[time entries report](#tag/reports/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1report~1time-entries/get)' for retrieving many time entries for different users and in an extensive date range.

parameters.name: user_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the user you want to receive time entries for.
Defaults to [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get) when the parameter is not provided on the request.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch time entries of other users, make sure you have access to the `team` scope and you are an `administrator`.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description:
The day you want to receive the user's time entries for. The format is: `Y-m-d`.
Defaults to today when the parameter is not provided on the request.

Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

time_entries.description: The time entries for the user on the selected day.

403.description: You are not allowed to retrieve the time entries

post.description:
Creating a new time entry functions in a specific way depending on your organisation's `timesheet` feature setting. When you
have set this feature to `times`, you create time entries with a `start` and `end` time in a timeline. When you try to create such
a time entry, the response will always result in either an error or a `201` (describing the newly created entry).

When your organisation's `timesheet` feature setting is set to `hours`, it can be that a new entry is created (`201`) or an existing entry is modified (`200`) when you try to create one.
Keeping will make sure you will not have multiple entries with the exact same details when your `timesheet` is set to `hours`.
So when you create a new entry for two hours with the exact same details as an already existing entry with three hours,
Keeping will adjust the existing entry and increment its hours to five.

When you have either `projects` or `tasks` enabled in your organisation you are **required** to provide a valid `project_id` or `task_id` respectively.

If your organisation uses breaks and times you should take extra care interpreting the data of the created entry because they could differ from the data you provided in the request body.
Read the documentation of the `purpose` parameter for more information.

time_entry.description:
The modified time entry that already exists. You than receive a `200` when the timesheet is set to `hours` and most of your
submitted properties exactly match that of an existing entry.

For example, when you create a new entry for two hours with the exact same details as an already existing entry with three hours,
Keeping will adjust the existing entry and increment its hours to five.

created_additional_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where created as well next to the entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when your new create a `break`, or the created entry was wrapped around an existing `break`.

modified_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where modified by the creation of the new entry. This can happen when you create a `break`,
or an ongoing entry is stopped by the creation of another ongoing entry.

deleted_existing_time_entry_ids.description: A list of time entries that where deleted by the creation of the new entry. This can happen when you create a `break`.

created_additional_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where created as well next to the entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when your new create a `break`, or the created entry was wrapped around an existing `break`.

modified_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where modified by the creation of the new entry. This can happen when you create a `break`,
or an ongoing entry is stopped by the creation of another ongoing entry.

deleted_existing_time_entry_ids.description: A list of time entries that where deleted by the creation of the new entry. This can happen when you create a `break`.

403.description: You are not allowed to create this time entry

get.description:
Fetch the last time entry that satisfies some specific predicate.
For example you can fetch the last finished entry that can be resumed of a specific project using the parameters `?project_ids[]={project_id}&ongoing=false&locked=false`.
Or you could fetch the last ongoing entry using the parameter `?ongoing=true`.

Please only use this to lookup data for suggestions and ongoing indicators, but **not** to search for many entries at once.
You can use '[time entries report](#tag/reports/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1report~1time-entries/get)' for retrieving many time entries for different users and in a large date range.

parameters.name: user_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the user you want to receive time entries for.
Defaults to [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get) when the parameter is not provided on the request.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch time entries of other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: If you only want the last entry on a specific date. Otherwise Keeping will consider all the user's time entries.

parameters.name: purpose
parameters.description: If you only want the last entry of a specific purpose.

parameters.name: project_ids
parameters.description: If you only want the last entry that is linked to **one of several** projects. You can specify the project ids, even if its just one. This parameter is only considered when `projects` are enabled. If the project does not exist, Keeping will respond with a `404`.

parameters.name: client_ids
parameters.description: If you only want the last entry that is linked to **one of several** clients. You can specify the client ids, even if its just one. This parameter is only considered when `projects` are enabled. If the client does not exist Keeping will respond with a `404`.

parameters.name: task_ids
parameters.description: If you only want the last entry that is linked to **one of several** tasks. You can specify the task ids, even if its just one. This parameter is only considered when `tasks` are enabled. If the task does not exist Keeping will respond with a `404`.

parameters.name: external_reference_ids
parameters.description:
If you only want the last entry that is linked to **one of several** external references.
You can specify the external reference ids, even if its just one.
If the external reference id does not exist within Keeping it will be ignored.

parameters.name: external_reference_types
parameters.description: If you only want the last entry that is linked to **one of several** external references types. You can specify the external reference types, even if its just one.

parameters.name: ongoing
parameters.description: If you are only interested in the currently ongoing entry you should set this value to `true`, and if you want to ignore ongoing entries set this value to `false`.

parameters.name: locked
parameters.description: If you want to ignore locked entries, set this value to `false`.

time_entry.description: The last entry of the user that satisfies the provided criteria.

403.description: You are not allowed to retrieve the entry

parameters.name: entry_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the entry you want to fetch.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch or modify time entries of other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

403.description: You are not allowed access

patch.description:
Modify an existing time entry.
You should `PATCH` the entry, i.e. send along only the properties you wish to change in the request body.
You can leave out the properties you do not wish to modify.

Please note that you cannot modify locked time entries, and trying to do so will result in a `403`.

When your organisation's `timesheet` feature setting is set to `hours`, it can be that an entry is collapsed into the entry you are modifying, and is deleted.
This is because Keeping will make sure you do not have multiple entries with the exact same details when your `timesheet` is set to `hours`.
So when you modify an entry of two hours to have the exact same details as an already existing entry with three hours,
Keeping will collapse the three hour long entry into the two hour long entry and increment its hours to five.
Afterwards the three hour long entry is deleted, so will be added to `meta.deleted_existing_time_entry_ids`.

### Overlap prevention for breaks
If your organisation uses `breaks` and `times` you should take extra care interpreting the details of the modified entry
since they could be different from the details provided in the request body.

When your organisation's timesheet type is set to `times` breaks can exhibit **special behaviour** which prevents them
from overlapping with time entries with another purpose.

You can modify breaks such that it would overlap with `work` time entries.
However, Keeping will automatically adjust the existing time entries so that the break is modified according to your specification without any overlap.
When other entries get modified or deleted because of a changed break,
Keeping will notify you using `meta.modified_existing_time_entry_ids` or `meta.deleted_existing_time_entry_ids` respectively.

Breaks can overlap with other breaks but will be marked with an alert in Keeping's interface.

When you modify a time entry that is not a `break` and there already exists a break which will overlap with that entry,
then Keeping will adjust the `start` or `end` of your modified entry to prevent overlap.
Keeping could also split your modified entry in multiple entries to prevent overlap,
in which case you receive one of them in `time_entry` and references to the others in `meta.created_additional_time_entry_ids`.
If the modification of a time entry is impossible due to the full overlap with a break,
or if the modification would require you to adjust a locked entry, you will receive a `403`.

parameters.name: entry_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the entry you want to update.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch or modify time entries of other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

created_additional_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where created as well next to the entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when you modify a `break`, or modified an entry that was wrapped around an existing `break`.

modified_existing_time_entry_ids.description: A list of time entries that where modified by the modification of the entry referenced in `time_entry`. This can happen when you modify a `break`.

deleted_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where deleted by the modification of the entry referenced in `time_entry`. This can happen when you create a `break`,
or when several entries are collapsed into a single entry on the `hours` timesheet because they now have the exact same details.
Entries will always get collapsed into the entry you are trying to modify.

403.description: You are not allowed to update this time entry

delete.description: Permanently delete a time entry. Please note that you cannot delete locked time entries, and trying to do so will result in a `403`.

parameters.name: entry_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the entry you want to destroy.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch or modify time entries of other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

403.description: You are not allowed to delete this entry

post.description:
Is a special variation of [time entry creation](#tag/entries/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1time-entries/post).
Uses all the details from the entry referenced by the path parameter `{entry_id}`
to result in a (new or modified) ongoing entry on the current day, i.e. today.
Keeping will indicate a new entry with a `201` status code, and a modified entry with a `200` status code.

Could result in a newly created ongoing entry on the current day,
with the exact same details as the entry referenced by the path parameter `{entry_id}`. Or, could result
in a modified entry in specific situations when you using the `hours` timesheet.

Please note that you cannot resume locked time entries, and trying to do so will result in a `403`.

If your organisation uses `breaks` and `times` you should take extra care interpreting the details of the resulting entry.
Read the documentation of the `purpose` parameter on the [create request](#tag/entries/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1time-entries/post) and the documentation of the response schema under `meta` for more information.

parameters.name: entry_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the entry you want to use as a blueprint for the resumed entry.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch or modify time entries of other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

time_entry.description:
The modified time entry. A `200` will occur if your organsiation's timesheet type is set to `hours`.
 When you are resuming a time entry for today, Keeping will modify that exact entry, and the `time_entry.id` will correspond
 with the path parameter `{entry_id}`. However, it could be that you are resuming an entry from an earlier date, which
 resumed a different entry that was created on the current day, therefore make sure to check `time_entry.id`.

created_additional_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where created as well next to the modified entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when an ongoing entry was stopped which wrapped it around an existing `break`.

modified_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where modified by the modified entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when another ongoing entry is stopped by this action.

deleted_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where deleted by the modified entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when an ongoing `break` entry was stopped by this action.

resume.summary: Resume an entry on the `hours` timesheet

time_entry.description:
The newly created ongoing entry.
Please note that the `time_entry.id` will be different from the provided path parameter `{entry_id}`.

created_additional_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where created as well next to the created entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when you resume a `break` and this action splits entries of another purpose.

modified_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where modified by the created entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when another ongoing entry is stopped by this action.

deleted_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where deleted by the created entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when an ongoing `break` entry was stopped by this action.

resume.summary: Resume an entry on the `times` timesheet

403.description: You are not allowed to resume this time entry

patch.description:
Is a special variation of '[update time entry](#tag/entries/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1time-entries~1{entry_id}/patch)'.
Just modifies the referenced time entry in such a way that it is stopped at the moment the request is received by the server.

When your organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, this corresponds to updating with the current time set in the `end` parameter.

When your organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, this corresponds to updating the `hours` parameter and incrementing it with the time passed since the `start` of the ongoing entry. Therefor, you do not have to calculate the difference.
 
Please note that you can only stop an ongoing time entry.

If your organisation uses `breaks` and `times` you should take extra care interpreting the data of the resulting entry.
Read the documentation of the [update request](#tag/entries/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1time-entries~1{entry_id}/patch) and the documentation of the response schema under `meta` for more information.

parameters.name: entry_id
parameters.description:
The `id` of the entry you want to stop.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only receive time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).
If you want to fetch or modify time entries of other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

time_entry.description:
The modified time entry, of which the `time_entry.id` will correspond with the provided path parameter `{entry_id}`.
Please note that this is not always true when [resuming an entry](#tag/entries/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1time-entries~1{entry_id}~1resume/post).

created_additional_time_entry_ids.description: A list of time entries that where created as well next to the stopped entry referenced in `time_entry`.

modified_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where modified by the stopped entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when you stop a `break`.

deleted_existing_time_entry_ids.description:
A list of time entries that where deleted by the stopped entry referenced in `time_entry`.
This can happen when you stop a `break`.

403.description: You are not allowed to stop this time entry

get.description:
Retrieve an overview of all the hours worked that meet some specific criteria.
For example, you can:
 - Get all hours for the entire year grouped by users
 - Get the hours in a particular project within a particular week grouped by tasks
 - Get the hours a user worked on a specific task in the entire year, grouped by weeks

parameters.name: from
parameters.description:
