The from date of the period the report should describe. This date is included in the period.
Make sure its the same or earlier than the `to` date, and the difference in days is not greater than 366 days.
Perform multiple requests if you want to fetch data for longer periods.
The format is: `Y-m-d`.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

parameters.name: to
parameters.description:
The to date of the period the report should describe. This date is included in the period.
Make sure its the same or later than the `from` date, and the difference in days is not greater than 366 days.
Perform multiple requests if you want to fetch data for longer periods.
The format is: `Y-m-d`.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

parameters.name: row_type
parameters.description:
Select by which type of item the hours should be grouped.
If you want an item in the response to represent the hours worked on a specific project you should choose `project`, etc.

You can only choose `project` and `client` if `projects` are enabled.
You can only choose `task` if `tasks` are enabled.
You can only choose `external_reference` and `external_reference_types` if the organisation at least has subscription plan `plus_2019`.

parameters.name: user_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries from **these** users.
If you want to retrieve the hours for all users, leave out this parameter.
Not finding one of the users will result in a `404`.

When your own user is a `team_member`, this parameter is considered **required** and **should** contain the single id of
your user: `?user_ids[]={your_user_id}`. Do not add additional user id's in this situation, otherwise you will receive a `403` response.

parameters.name: project_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries with **these** projects.
Not finding one of the projects will result in a `404`.
If you want to retrieve the hours for all projects, including for time entries that do not have a associated project, leave out this parameter.
You can only use this parameter if `projects` are enabled.

parameters.name: client_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries for **these** clients.
Not finding one of the clients will result in a `404`.
If you want to retrieve the hours for all clients, including for time entries that do not have a associated client, leave out this parameter.
You can only use this parameter if `projects` are enabled.

parameters.name: task_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries with **these** tasks.
Not finding one of the tasks will result in a `404`.
If you want to retrieve the hours for all tasks, including for time entries that do not have a associated task, leave out this parameter.
You can only use this parameter if `tasks` are enabled.

parameters.name: tag_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries with **these** tags.
Not finding one of the tags will result in a `404`.
If you want to retrieve the hours for all tags, including for time entries that do not have a associated tag, leave out this parameter.
You can only use this parameter if `tags` are enabled.

parameters.name: external_reference_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries with **these** external references.
If one of the external references is not found it will be ignored.
If you want to retrieve the hours for all external references, including for time entries that do not have a external references, leave out this parameter.
You can only choose this parameter if the organisation at least has subscription plan `plus_2019`.

parameters.name: external_reference_types
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include hours of time entries with **these** external references types.
If one of the types is invalid we will return a `422`.
For example, if you only want to see hours associated with `github_issue`s or `jira_issue`s you can do so using this parameter.

If you want to retrieve the hours for all types, including for time entries that do not have a external references, leave out this parameter.
You can only choose this parameter if the organisation at least has subscription plan `plus_2019`.

403.description: You are not allowed access to this report

get.description:
Retrieve time entries that satisfy a specific predicate determined by its parameters.
For example, use this if you want to get the specific time entries of multiple users all at once.

This result is **paginated**, so you will need to send the server multiple requests if you want to
retrieve a large dataset of specific time entries.
Please take our [usage limitations](#section/General-usage/Usage-limitations) and
the [usage etiquette](#section/General-usage/Usage-etiquette) into account when doing so.

The format is: `Y-m-d`.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

parameters.name: to
parameters.description:
The to date of the period the report should describe. This date is included in the period.
Make sure its the same or later than the `from` date.
The format is: `Y-m-d`.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

parameters.name: user_ids

Filter the result to only include time entries from **these** users.
If you want to retrieve the time entries of all users, leave out this parameter.
Not finding one of the users will result in a `404`.

When your own user is a `team_member` this parameter is **required** and **should** contain the single id of

If you want to retrieve the time entries of all clients, including the ones that do not have an associated client, leave out this parameter.

parameters.name: task_ids

Filter the result to only include time entries with **these** tasks.
Not finding one of the tasks will result in a `404`.
If you want to retrieve the time entries of all tasks, including the ones that do not have an associated task, leave out this parameter.
You can only use this parameter if `tasks` are enabled.

parameters.name: tag_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include time entries with **these** tags.
Not finding one of the tags will result in a `404`.
If you want to retrieve the time entries of all tags, including the ones that do not have an associated tag, leave out this parameter.
You can only use this parameter if `tags` are enabled.

parameters.name: external_reference_ids
parameters.description:
Filter the result to only include time entries with **these** external references.
If you want to retrieve the time entries of all external references, including the ones that do not have external references, leave out this parameter.
If one of the external references is not found it will be ignored.

If you want to retrieve the time entries of all types, including the ones that do not have external references, leave out this parameter.
For example, if you only want to see time entries associated to `github_issue` or `jira_issue` you can do so using this parameter.

get.description:
In order to manage all your organisation's projects your user should be an `administrator`. Otherwise this will
only return projects where your own user is participating in, or has ever created a time entry with.

Use to fetch all the valid project options when you are considering creating a new time entry. 
In this case you should provide a `user_id`, e.g. use the URL parameters
`?user_id={the_user_id_you_want_to_create_an_entry_for}&state[]=active` to find all the available valid projects.
This is the primary reason projects are also accessible **without** the `project_management` scope.

The response is **paginated**, so you will need to send the server multiple requests if you want to
retrieve all projects when there are more than `100`.

parameters.name: state
parameters.description:
Use this parameter to access archived projects. By default Keeping will only return active projects.
You can access both archived and active projects at once by providing them as an array, e.g. `?state[]=active&state[]=archived`.

parameters.name: task_id
parameters.description:

When not provided Keeping will return the projects of for all tasks, including the ones without task assignments.

parameters.name: user_id
parameters.description:
Filter the projects down to the ones that are participated in by this specific user.

When not provided Keeping will return the projects for all users, including the ones without participations.

If your own user is a `team_member` or does not have access to the `team` scope
this parameter can only contain the `id` of [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get).

parameters.name: client_id
parameters.description:

When not provided Keeping will return the projects for all clients, including the ones without a client.

One is considered `inactive` when it only has archived projects linked to it.
You can access both active and inactive clients at once by providing them as an array, e.g. `?state[]=active&state[]=inactive`.

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific client.

name.description: The unique name of the client.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It is unique not only among clients but also tasks, projects and users.

state.description: A created client starts out as an `active` client, but can be change to `inactive` when it has only archived projects associated with it.

name.description: The name of the client. This **should** be unique among all clients within your organisation's Keeping environment.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It **should** be unique within your entire organisation, not only among clients but also projects, tasks and users.

name.description: The name of the client. This **should** be unique among all clients within your organisation's Keeping environment.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It **should** be unique within your entire organisation, not only among clients but also projects, tasks and users.

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific time entry.

user_id.description: Identifier of the user whose time entry this is.

date.description:
Date of the time entry. Time entries are always associated with specific date irrespective of a specific time zone.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

purpose.description:
This describes the purpose of time entry. Regular time entries are `work` time entries.

Time entries with the purpose `break` are a special type of time entry that do not count towards the total
amount of worked time, and are reported on separately. You can only create or modify these entries when `breaks` are
enabled for your organisation.

project_id.description:
The identifier of the project this entry is describing time worked for.
Can be `null` when `projects` are/were disabled for the entire organisation. Will always be `null` for time entries with the `break` purpose.

task_id.description:
The identifier of the task this entry is describing time worked on.
Can be `null` when `tasks` are/were disabled for the entire organisation. Will always be `null` for time entries with the `break` purpose.

tag_ids.description:
The identifiers of the tags this entry is describing time worked on.
Can be `[]` when `tags` are/were disabled for the entire organisation. Will always be `[]` for time entries with the `break` purpose.

note.description: An optional note describing what the user did during the time this entry represents. Can be `null`.

external_references.description:
Used to refer to data in external application. A single time entry can be linked to multiple external references.
They will show up in Keeping's timesheet, including a URL which the user can directly use to go to the external data.

id.description: Unique identifier of a specific data point in an external application.

type.description: Describes the application this reference is linking to. When an entry is linked to an invoice or payslip it will automatically be `locked`.

name.description: Name to describe the data point from the external application.

url.description: URL linking to the data point from the external application.

start.description:
Describes the start time of the time entry including the date and the time zone.
Its date mostly corresponds to the `date` property of the entry, but can be different in specific cases when the time zone was adjusted afterwards.
The start can be `null` when the organisation's timesheet type is/was set to `hours`.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information on the date time format.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours` **and** the entry is `ongoing`, this value will **not** be `null`, but represent the last time the entry was set to ongoing.
In this case you should **ONLY** use this value for calculating the up-to-date hours value.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `time` you can display the start time to your users.

When a time entry is ongoing you should calculate the difference between the current time and the time in `start` and add it to the `hours` to get at the up-to-date hours the time entry is tracking.
Make sure to [account for potential differences between the server time and your device time](#section/General-usage/Server-time) if you want your calculated time to match the time in Keeping's UI exactly.

end.description:
Describes the end time of the time entry including the date and the time zone.
Its date mostly corresponds to the `date` property of the entry, but can be different in specific cases when the time zone was adjusted afterwards.
Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information on the date time format.

When the organisation's timesheet type is/was set to `hours` this value will be `null`.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `time` you can display the start time to your users. If the entry is `ongoing` in this case, the end will be `null` as well.

hours.description:
This is the time entry's confirmed value expressed in hours.
It does **not** include the ongoing time since it was last started.
This you as a developer should calculate at the client-side.

Minutes are expressed in the decimal points where each minute is roughly `0.0167` hours.

In order to find the total time from a collection of entries you should sum the `hours` value of each entry.
In general entries with the `break` purpose should not be counted when calculating the total amount of hours.

The value does not account for a currently ongoing entry.
When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `times` this merely reflects the amount of time between `start` and `end`, and will be `null` in the case of an ongoing entry.
When the timesheet type is set to `hours` this value can be `null` for a newly created ongoing entry, or not `null` if an entry was resumed after it already accumulated some time.

ongoing.description:
Indicates the time entry is currently tracking, i.e. the stop watch is running. A user can only have a single running entry at a time.

When a time entry is ongoing you should calculate the difference between the current time and the time in `start` and add it to the `hours` to get at the up-to-date hours the time entry is tracking.
Make sure to [account for potential differences between the server time and your device time](#section/General-usage/Server-time) if you want your calculated time to match the time in Keeping's UI exactly.

locked.description:
Indicates the time entry is locked and cannot be modified or deleted. A time entry can be locked when its project or
task has been archived, when it has an invoice or payslip associated with it or when its purpose is `break` but breaks
are disabled.

is_direct_hours.description: Indicates the time entry is direct or indirect hours. This depends on the task assignment or the project's direct hours setting.

user_id.description:
The `id` of the user you want to create a time entry for. Defaults to [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get) when the property is not provided on the request.

If you do not have access to the `team` scope you can only create time entries for [your own user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1me/get). If you want to create time entries for other users, make sure your access token grants you access to the `team` scope and your user has the `administrator` role.

Once a time entry is created you cannot change its `user_id`.

When the organisation's timesheet is `hours`, Keeping will use the `user_id` as one of the distinguishing properties when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

date.description:
The day on which you want to create a time entry, irrespective of the time zone.
There is a single exception, your organisation's time zone does matter for the default value when the property is not provided on the request.
In which case, the value will default to the current day in your organisation's time zone.

The format is: `Y-m-d`. Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

Once a time entry is created you cannot change its `date`.

When the organisation's timesheet is `hours`, Keeping will use the `date` as one of the distinguishing properties when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

purpose.description:
The purpose describes what type of time entry you want to create. Regular time entries are `work` time entries.
When your organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, these types of entries can overlap with each other but will be marked with an alert in
Keeping's interface.

Time entries of the type `break` can only be created when `breaks` are enabled for your entire organisation. Breaks are
a special type of time entry that do not count towards the total amount of worked time, and are reported on separately.

Once a time entry is created you cannot change its `purpose`.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `purpose` as one of the distinguishing properties when
it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

### Overlap prevention for breaks
When your organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, breaks can exhibit **special behaviour** which prevents them
from overlapping with time entries that have another purpose.

You can create breaks that would overlap with other time entries.
However, Keeping will then automatically adjust the existing time entries so that the break is created according to your specification without any overlap.
When other entries get modified or deleted because of a new break,
Keeping will notify you using `meta.modified_existing_time_entry_ids` or `meta.deleted_existing_time_entry_ids` respectively.

Breaks can overlap with other breaks but will be marked with an alert in Keeping's interface.

When you create a time entry with a different purpose and there already is a break that would overlap with the new entry,
Keeping will adjust the `start` or `end` of your new entry as to prevent overlap with the break.
Keeping will also split your new entry in multiple entries if necessary,
in which case you receive one in `time_entry` and references to the others in `meta.created_additional_time_entry_ids`.
If the creation of a time entry is impossible due to the full overlap with a break,
or if the creation would require you to adjust a locked entry, you will receive a `403`.

project_id.description:
When your organisation has `projects` enabled, you will be **required** to provide the `project_id` of an **active** project of which the user (described by `user_id`) is a **participant**, when creating a time entry with the purpose `work`.
If `projects` are disabled for your organisation you can leave out this property.

Parameter can be left out of the request when creating a `break` time entry.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `project_id` as one of the distinguishing properties
when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

task_id.description:
When your organisation has `tasks` enabled, you will be **required** to provide a `task_id` when creating a time entry with the purpose `work`. In case `projects` are enabled as well, the task should be **active** and currently
**associated** with the project you specified using `project_id`. When `projects` are disabled you can enter the `task_id` of any **active** task.
If `tasks` are disabled for your organisation you can leave out this property.

Parameter can be left out of the request when creating a `break` time entry.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `task_id` as one of the distinguishing properties
when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

note.description:
An **optional** note you can add to your time entry, you can use it to describe the entry itself.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `note` as one of the distinguishing properties when
it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created. Keeping will make sure to trim whitespace from the note when doing this.

tag_ids.description:
The identifiers of the tags this entry is describing time worked on.
Can be `[]` when `tags` are/were disabled for the entire organisation. Will always be `[]` for time entries with the `break` purpose.

external_references.description:
If you want to link your time entry to some external data you can use this property to store the link. A single time entry can be linked to multiple external references.
They will show up in Keeping's timesheet, including a URL which the user can directly use to go to the external data.

![Example of external reference](/images/developer/external-reference.png "Example of external reference")

Keeping supports links to various project management applications.
You yourself cannot create links to these applications but you can create an external reference of type `generic_work_reference` for your own application.
Generic work references are displayed within Keeping with a link icon, and can link to any application.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `external_references` as one of the distinguishing properties
when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

id.description:
Provide a hash in hex format you yourself can use to uniquely identify the external reference.
You could use a hashing algorithm such as [MD5](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/MD5) or [SHA-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SHA-1).
The fact that these algorithms are not secure enough for modern password hashing does not matter in our case.

If you attach an external reference with the same identifier to another entry Keeping will see them as a link to the exact same object.

For example if your application is called "My Cool App" and you want to link to a to-do with your internal identifier `1234`
you could use the resulting hash from `md5("my_cool_app.todo.1234")` as your identifier, i.e. `d69e192e3827b90e9d13e888317113e1`.

type.description:
Using the API you will only be able to create external references of the type `generic_work_reference`. Other types exist
but are reserved for Keeping's internals.

name.description: Provide a name for the external item to display within Keeping's UI.

url.description: Provide a valid URL to the item within your application. An invalid URL will result in a `422`.

start.description:
Can only be used if the organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, it will be ignored otherwise.

Defaults to the current time (in your organisation's time zone) when not provided.

This property accepts the time in almost all formats and handles them gracefully.
Both 24-hour and AM/PM formats are accepted, so [PHP date formats](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.date.php):
`g:ia` (e.g. `1:15pm`) and `G:i` (e.g. `13:15`) are both accepted.
You should **not** provide the full date, only a time is accepted by this property.

If this property is left out of the response Keeping will assume the current time, even for entries on earlier dates.
The `start` property is **required** for entries on future dates.

end.description:
Can only be used if the organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, it will be ignored otherwise.

This property accepts the time in almost all formats and handles them gracefully.
Both 24-hour and AM/PM formats are accepted, so [PHP date formats](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.date.php):
`g:ia` (e.g. `1:15pm`) and `G:i` (e.g. `13:15`) are both accepted.
You should **not** provide the full date, only a time is accepted by this property.

If this property is left out of the response and `start` is in the past you will create an ongoing time entry.
The `end` property is **required** for entries on future dates.

A user can only have a single running entry, so when you create an ongoing entry while there already was one, Keeping will
stop the previous ongoing entry.
This will be reflected in the `meta.modified_existing_time_entry_ids`.

Keeping assumes the `end` always to be exactly the same or later than the `start` time.
This means it is possible to have entries end the next day. For example, you could have an entry that starts at `23:00` in the
 evening and end at `4:00` in the morning. In this case the entry still belongs to the `date` the entry was started on.

hours.description:
Can only be used if the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, it will be ignored otherwise.

If this property is left out of the response you will create an ongoing time entry.
Otherwise you will create a finished time entry with an exact amount of hours.

A user only has a single running entry, so when you create an ongoing entry while there already was one, Keeping will
stop the previous ongoing entry.
This will be reflected in the `meta.modified_existing_time_entry_ids`.

project_id.description:
When your organisation has `projects` enabled, you can provide the `project_id` of an **active** project of which the entrie's user is a **participant**.
If `projects` are disabled for your organisation you can leave out this property.

`break` time entries cannot have an assigned project.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `project_id` as one of the distinguishing properties
when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

task_id.description:
When your organisation has `tasks` enabled, you can provide a `task_id`. In case `projects` are enabled as well, the task should be **active** and currently
**associated** with the project you specified using `project_id`. When `projects` are disabled, you can enter the `task_id` of any **active** task.
If `tasks` are disabled for your organisation you can leave out this property.

`break` time entries cannot have an assigned task.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `task_id` as one of the distinguishing properties
when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

note.description:
An **optional** note you can add to your time entry, you can use it to describe the entry itself.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `note` as one of the distinguishing properties when
it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created. Keeping will make sure to trim whitespace from the note when doing this.

tag_ids.description:
The identifiers of the tags this entry is describing time worked on.
Can be `[]` when `tags` are/were disabled for the entire organisation. Will always be `[]` for time entries with the `break` purpose.

external_references.description:
If you want to link you time entry to some external data you can use this property to store the link. A single time entry can be linked to multiple external references.
They will show up in Keeping's timesheet, including a URL which the user can directly use to go to the external data.

![Example of external reference](/images/developer/external-reference.png "Example of external reference")

Keeping supports links to various project management applications. You yourself cannot create links to these applications but you can create a `generic_work_reference` for your own application.
You can however keep links of other external reference types that already exist. You do so by submitting the exact same external reference data that you received when retrieving the time entry on modification.
When you leave out existing external references from the array they will be deleted.

If you do not include `external_references` in your request as a property they will not be modified. To delete all of them provide an emtpy array.

When the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, Keeping will use the `external_references` as one of the distinguishing properties
when it looks if an entry needs to be modified instead of created.

id.description:
Provide a hash in hex format you yourself can use to uniquely identify the external reference.
You could use a hashing algorithm such as [MD5](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/MD5).
The fact that these algorithms are not secure enough for modern password hashing does not matter in our case.

If you attach an external reference with the same identifier to another entry Keeping will consider them as a link to the exact same object.

For example if your application is called "My Cool App" and you want to link to a to-do with your internal identifier `1234`
you could use the resulting hash from `md5("my_cool_app.todo.1234")` as your identifier, i.e. `d69e192e3827b90e9d13e888317113e1`.

type.description:
Using the API you will only be able to create external references of the type `generic_work_reference`. Other types exist
but are reserved for Keeping's internals.

You **cannot** change a external reference's type after it has been created.

name.description:
Provide a name for the external item to display within Keeping's UI.
Is **required** for new external references.

url.description: Provide a valid URL to the item within your application. An invalid URL will result in a `422`.

start.description:
Can only be used if the organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, it will be ignored otherwise.

You can only enter start times that are in the past if you want to modify an ongoing entry.

This property accepts the time in almost all formats and handles them gracefully.
Both 24-hour and AM/PM formats are accepted, so [PHP date formats](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.date.php):
`g:ia` (e.g. `1:15pm`) and `G:i` (e.g. `13:15`) are both accepted.
You should **not** provide the full date, only a time is accepted by this property.

end.description:
Can only be used if the organisation's timesheet type is set to `times`, it will be ignored otherwise.

Can be left out for ongoing entries. If you want to stop an ongoing entry on a specific time, you should submit a valid `end` time and the entry will be stopped.

This property accepts the time in almost all formats and handles them gracefully.
Both 24-hour and AM/PM formats are accepted, so [PHP date formats](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.date.php):
`g:ia` (e.g. `1:15pm`) and `G:i` (e.g. `13:15`) are both accepted.
You should **not** provide the full date, only a time is accepted by this property.

Keeping assumes the `end` always to be exactly the same or later than the `start` time.
This means it is possible to have entries end the next day. For example, you could have an entry that starts at `23:00` in the
 evening and end at `4:00` in the morning. In this case the entry still belongs to the `date` the entry was started on.

hours.description:
Can only be used if the organisation's timesheet type is set to `hours`, it will be ignored otherwise.

If the entry is ongoing and you submit `hours` it will automatically be stopped.
When a time entry is ongoing you should calculate the difference between the current time and the time in `start` and add it to the `hours` to get at the up-to-date hours the time entry is tracking, if you want to offset the hours including the ongoing time.
You can also use '[stop a time entry](#tag/entries/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1time-entries~1{entry_id}~1stop/patch)' to stop ongoing entries and increment the `hours` correctly without you having to make a calculation.

message.description: Description of the error that can be used to display a message to the user. If possible, this message will be localized in accordance with the `Accept-Language` header value. Data from this error description is not suitable for use in error handling logic other than displaying a message to the user, and should always be considered subject to change.

error.description:
The error object describing what went wrong.

Please do **not** assume the existence of a JSON encoded error message, it could very well be that our load balancer returns a HTML document or something else entirely. So when handling error responses, first check the status code and only if present look at this JSON encoded error message for more information.

expires_in.description: Describes the amount of seconds the access token will be valid. Make sure to calculate the exact moment of expiration yourself to prevent unnecessary requests to the web service.

access_token.description: The access token you can now send along with further requests in the `Authorization` header to authenticate yourself. The header value should be: `Bearer {access_token}`. This token conforms to the [JWT format](https://jwt.io/).

refresh_token.description: When the access token has expired you should request a new one using this refresh token.

error.description: String representing the type of error, which can be used for programmatically handling specific errors.

error_description.description: Human readable description of the error meant for the developer. This string will **not** be localized.
error_description.example: The request is missing a required parameter, includes an invalid parameter value, includes a parameter more than once, or is otherwise malformed.

hint.description: A hint that could point the developer in the direction of solving the problem. This string will **not** be localized.

grant_type.description: The grant type you wish to use to request a new access token.

client_id.description: Your OAuth application's client id provided by Keeping to you as a developer.

client_secret.description: Your OAuth application's client secret provided by Keeping to you as a developer. Make sure to keep this value secret.

redirect_uri.description: The exact redirect URL you registered with Keeping for your OAuth application. This could be a URL with the `https` scheme or a custom scheme.

code.description: The authorisation code you received as a URL parameter (`?code=…`) when the user was directed back to your application's `redirect_uri`.

refresh_token.description: The refresh token you received with your last access token.

scope.description: Specify the scopes you wish to use for your new access token. You can only request fewer scopes than you did on your last access token request. When using multiple chain them using the `+` character, e.g. `time+team`. _Please note: do not use `%2B` to chain scopes._

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific organisation.

name.description: The organisation's name you setup in the Keeping settings.

url.description: The URL you can use to access the organisation's Keeping environment.

current_plan.description: The current Keeping plan the entire organisation is subscribed to. Read [the section on authorisation](#section/Authentication/Authorisation) for more information.

features.description: A collection of feature toggles on how the organisation uses Keeping. Read [the section on authorisation](#section/Authentication/Authorisation) for more information.

timesheet.description: Describes the type of timesheet that is currently enabled for your organisation. The `times` timesheet has time entries with a start and end so you have can have an exact trail of everyone work on any specific day. The `hours` timesheet allows you to enter hours per day, irrespective of the exact moment the work took place.

projects.description: Tells you whether or not **projects and clients** are enabled for the organisation. An administrator is allowed to disable them for the entire organisation. In the case projects are disabled you will not be required to specify a project when creating an entry, you neither can request projects or group reports on projects.

tasks.description: Tells you whether or not tasks are enabled for the organisation. An administrator is allowed to disable them for the entire organisation. In the case tasks are disabled you will not be required to specify a task when creating an entry, you neither can request tasks or group reports on tasks.

breaks.description: Tells you whether or not breaks are enabled for the organisation. Breaks can only be enabled for organisations that are on the `plus_2019` or `enterprise_2019` plan. Breaks allow you to create time entries with the purpose: `break`. Time entries with this `purpose` do not count towards the total worked hours, and Keeping will make sure they never overlap with time entries with another purpose.

time_zone.description: The current time zone used by default in the entire organisation. The string format is that of the [tz database](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database). Please [read the section on dates and time zones](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

currency.description: The currency used by default in the entire organisation. This is the currency that should be used for monetary values in the reports. The value contains the [ISO 4217](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_4217) code of the currency.

total.description: Describes the total number of entities across all pages.

per_page.description: The number of entities per page defined by the `per_page` URL parameter.

current_page.description: The current page defined by the `page` URL parameter.

last_page.description: The last available page. `null` when there are no entities found and therefore no last page exists.

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific project.

client.description: The single client associated with this project. Within the Keeping UI projects are usually grouped by their client. A client **can** be `null` when the project is not specific to a client, this could be true for internal projects.

name.description: The unique name of the project.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It is unique not only among projects but also tasks, clients and users.

direct.description:
Describes if time entries with this project will be counted as direct.
This property will be `null` when your organisation does not have at least subscription plan `plus_2019`.

task_id.description: The primary key of the assigned task. Use the '[fetch task](#tag/tasks/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1tasks~1{task_id}/get)' to retrieve more information.

direct.description: If your project's `direct` property equals `is_direct_through_task_assignments` this will indicate whether the task-project combination should be counted as direct. This property will be `null` when your organisation does not have at least subscription plan `plus_2019`.

task_assignments.description:
Will be `null` when tasks are disabled.
When tasks are enabled, this property lists all tasks associated to this project including both `active` and `archived` tasks.
Please note, when both tasks and projects are enabled, you can only create time entries for an active assigned task with an active project.

user_id.description: The primary key of the participating user. Use the '[fetch user](#tag/users/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1users~1{userId}/get)' to retrieve more information.

participations.description: This property lists of all users participating in this project, this includes both `active` and users with other states. Please note, that only participating users can use this project to create time entries.

state.description: The current state of the project. Only `active` projects can be used to create new time entries. In order to archive a project use '[archive](#tag/projects/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1projects~1{project_id}~1archive/patch)', in order to reactivate an `archived` project use '[restore](#tag/projects/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1projects~1{project_id}~1restore/patch)'.

client_id.description: The `id` of the client you want this project to be associated with. You can also leave this property out or set it to `null` to indicate that it is not associated with any client, but rather an internal project.

name.description: The name of the project. This **should** be unique among all projects within your organisation's Keeping environment.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It **should** be unique within your entire organisation, not only among projects but also tasks, clients and users.

direct.description: Describes when the hours of time entries that use this project are considered direct. You can only provide this property when your organisation has at least subscription plan: `plus_2019`.

task_id.description: The `id` of the task linked to this project. This `id` should reference an existing task in this organisation. You **cannot** use the same `task_id` multiple times in the `task_assignments`.

direct.description: Describes if a time entry using this project and task combination is considered direct. You can only provide this property when the projects direct property is set to `is_direct_through_task_assignments` and when your organisation has at least subscription plan: `plus_2019`.

task_assignments.description:
Describes the tasks that can be used in conjunction with this project for creating new time entries.

You can only use this property when `tasks` are enabled.
When you leave out this property Keeping will automatically assign tasks to the project that are setup to be assigned to all new projects by default.

user_id.description: The `id` of the user participating in this project. This `id` should reference an existing user in this organisation. You **cannot** use the same `user_id` multiple times in the `participations`.

participations.description:
Describes the users that can use this project when creating new time entries.

When you leave out this property Keeping will automatically populate the project with participants that are setup to join all new projects by default.

client_id.description: The `id` of the client you want this project to be associated with. You can also leave this property out or set it to `null` to indicate that it is not associated with any client, but rather an internal project.

name.description: The name of the project. This **should** be unique among all projects within your organisation's Keeping environment.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It **should** be unique within your entire organisation, not only among projects but also tasks, clients and users.

direct.description: Describes when the hours of time entries that use this project are considered direct. You can only provide this property when your organisation has at least subscription plan: `plus_2019`.

task_id.description: The `id` of the task linked to this project. This `id` should reference an existing task in this organisation. You **cannot** use the same `task_id` multiple times in the `task_assignments`.

direct.description: Describes if a time entry using this project and task combination is considered direct. You can only provide this property when the projects direct property is set to `is_direct_through_task_assignments` and when your organisation has at least subscription plan: `plus_2019`.

task_assignments.description:
Describes the tasks that can be used in conjunction with this project for creating new time entries.

Make sure to submit **ALL** the task assignments you want the project to have after the update, or simple do not include the property to make no changes.
When you leave out a single task assignment Keeping will remove it from the project.
Therefor, nobody will be able to create new time entries using this task and project combination anymore.

You can only use this property when `tasks` are enabled.

user_id.description: The `id` of the user participating in this project. This `id` should reference an existing user in this organisation. You **cannot** use the same `user_id` multiple times in the `participations`.

participations.description:
Describes the users that can use this project when creating new time entries.

Make sure to submit **ALL** the participations you want the project to have after the update, or simple do not include the property to make no changes.
When you leave out a single participation Keeping will remove it from the project.
Therefore, its user will not be able to create new time entries for this project anymore.

from.description:
The start day for the period this report is concerning, irrespective of a specific time zone.
The response will include time entries with a `date` from this day onward.

When `from` is equal to `to` the report only concerns a single day. The difference between `from` and `to` will not be greater than 366 days.

Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

to.description:
The end day for the period this report is concerning, irrespective of a specific time zone.
The response will include time entries with a `date` up to and including this day.

When `from` is equal to `to` the report only concerns a single day. The difference between `from` and `to` will not be greater than 366 days.

Read the [section on dates](#section/General-usage/Dates-and-time-zones) for more information.

row_type.description:
A report will contain `rows` which represent the time worked on a specific row type.
Each of your report's rows will be representing some period or entity from the Keeping API depending on the row type selected.

For example, when you select `project` each row will correspond to a project, and its `id` should be seen as a `project_id`.
The `hours` on the row will be the total hours worked on the project in the report's period and constrained to the request parameters.

rows.description: These report rows represent some period or entity from the Keeping API depending on the `row_type` of this report.

id.description:
When the report's `row_type` is set to `project` this id should be seen as a `project_id`, and similarly for other entities such as `task`, `client` and `user` and `external_reference`.

In the case that the report's `row_type` is set to a period (e.g. `month`) this id will be of the format: `Y-m-d..Y-m-d` describing the period, or `Y-m-d` if the period is just comprised of a single day.

In the case of an `row_type` of `external_reference_type` the id corresponds to a single `external_reference_type`, e.g. `basecamp_3_todo`.

description.description:
A description of the report row. In some cases it will be dependant on the `Accept-Language` header.

For example, when the report's `row_type` is set to `project` it will describe the project with it's name, code and client.

url.description: The url of the `external_reference`. Is `null` for other row types.

hours.description:
The total hours worked on the item in the report's period and constrained to the request parameters. Where the row is described ty the `row_type`.

Hours worked do **not** include the hours of the time entries with the purpose `break`. 

For example, when the `row_type` is set to `user` this value will represent the total worked hours by a single user in the report's period and constrained to the request parameters.

direct_hours.description:
Only contains the direct hours for this row. Whether an hours is counted as direct or not depends on the projects or tasks associated with the underlying time entries. 
Using `direct_hours`, you can calculate the indirect hours by subtracting the direct hours from the total amount of `hours` of the row.

Will be `null` when your organisation does not have at least the subscription plan `plus_2019`.

total_hours.description:
The total hours worked on the report's period and constrained to the request parameters.

Will equal the summation of the `hours` of all the `rows` in this report.

Hours worked do **not** include the hours of the time entries with the purpose `break`.

total_direct_hours.description:
Only contains the direct hours for the entire report. Whether an hours is counted as direct or not depends on the projects or tasks associated with the underlying time entries. 
Using `total_direct_hours`, you can calculate the total indirect hours by subtracting the direct hours from the `total_hours` of the entire report.

Will equal the summation of the `direct_hours` of all the `rows` in this report.

Will be `null` when your organisation does not have at least the subscription plan `plus_2019`.

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific tag.

name.description: The unique name for the tag.

project_ids.description: This field is only populated when the tag configuration is set to "linked tags" and `projects` is activated. This array then contains all projects where this tag can be selected.

task_ids.description: This field is only populated when the tag configuration is set to "linked tags" and `tasks` is activated. This array then contains all tasks where this tag can be selected.

client_ids.description: This field is only populated when the tag configuration is set to "linked tags" and `clients` is activated. This array then contains all clients where this tag can be selected.

default_project_ids.description:
This field is only populated when `projects` are activated.
When one of the `projects` from the array is added to an entry, the tag must also be added by default.

default_task_ids.description:
This field is only populated when `tasks` are activated.
When one of the `tasks` from the array is added to an entry, the tag must also be added by default.

default_client_ids.description:
This field is only populated when `clients` are activated.
When one of the `clients` from the array is added to an entry, the tag must also be added by default.

locked_project_ids.description:
This field is only populated when `projects` are activated.
This field is an extension of `default_project_ids`. When this field is populated, the tag toe default is added to the entry cannot be deselected.

locked_task_ids.description:
This field is only populated when `tasks` are activated.
This field is an extension of `default_task_ids`. When this field is populated, the tag toe default is added to the entry cannot be deselected.

locked_client_ids.description:
This field is only populated when `clients` are activated.
This field is an extension of `default_client_ids`. When this field is populated, the tag toe default is added to the entry cannot be deselected.

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific task.

name.description: The unique name for the task.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It is unique not only among tasks but also projects, clients and users.

direct.description:
When `projects` are disabled, describes if time entries with this task will be counted as direct.

When `projects` are enabled, this property describes whether assignments to this task will be assumed to be direct by default.
This is especially relevant when the project's direct property is set to `is_direct_through_task_assignments` and no `task_assignments` are provided on project creation.

This property is `null` if your organisation does not have at least subscription plan `plus_2019`.

state.description: The current state of the task. Only `active` tasks can be used to create new time entries. In order to archive a task use '[archive](#tag/tasks/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1tasks~1{task_id}~1archive/patch)', in order to reactivate an `archived` task use '[restore](#tag/tasks/paths/~1{organisation_id}~1tasks~1{task_id}~1restore/patch)'.

name.description: The name of the task. This **should** be unique among all tasks within your organisation's Keeping environment.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It **should** be unique within your entire organisation, not only among tasks but also projects, clients and users.

direct.description:
When `projects` are enabled Keeping will assume new task assignments to be direct by default if this property is set to `true`.
When `projects` are disabled Keeping will assume all time entries associated with this task to be direct if this property is set to `true`.
You can only provide this property when your organisation has at least subscription plan: `plus_2019`.

name.description: The name of the task. This **should** be unique among all tasks within your organisation's Keeping environment.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It **should** be unique within your entire organisation, not only among tasks but also projects, clients and users.

direct.description:
When `projects` are enabled Keeping will assume new task assignments to be direct by default if this property is set to `true`.
When `projects` are disabled Keeping will assume all time entries associated with this task to be direct if this property is set to `true`.
You can only provide this property when your organisation has at least subscription plan: `plus_2019`.

id.description: Primary key, use this identifier to uniquely identify a specific user.

first_name.description: The user's first name, or first names.

surname.description:
The user's surname (i.e. last name) including a possible prefix.
Keeping has special logic to deal with the most common Dutch surname prefixes, and order users correctly by their last name.
So there is no need to attach surname prefixes at the end of the like: `Doorn, van`, you can just use `van Doorn`.

code.description:
A unique code that you can use internally. It can be used throughout Keeping to identify specific entities.
It is unique not only among users but also projects, clients and tasks.

role.description:
The role that indicates this users privileges. In general users with the role `team_member` can create time entries 
for themselves and view reports on all their own time entries. Users with the role `administrator` can create time
entries for everyone, view broad organisational reports, and manage projects, clients and tasks. More information
can be found in [the authorisation section](#section/Authentication/Authorisation).

state.description:
Describes the current state of the `user`. This state does not impair other users from manipulating the time entries of this user.
A `blocked` user can be considered archived and can't access Keeping. A `decoupled` user has left the organisation themselves and cannot access Keeping anymore, they should receive a new invite.
A user that has the state `needs_invite` should first be invited before they can access Keeping.
An `invited` user has received an invite but still needs to accept it.
Only `active` and `inactive` users can actually use Keeping themselves.
Users that are `inactive` have not accessed Keeping for at least 180 days.

organisation_id.name: organisation_id
organisation_id.description: The `id` of the organisation you want to use in this action. See [the section on organisations](#tag/organisations) for more information.

page.name: page
page.description: Since this request is paginated you can provide the page number you want to request, if you want to see beyond page `1`. Please consult `meta.last_page` to see the maximum page number.

per_page.name: per_page
per_page.description: If you want to see more results on a single page you can increase the amount of items shown per page.

auth.description: Below follows a schematic description of the authentication flows referenced by API URLs that require authentication.

scopes.team: Access data of the entire team, not only data of the authorizer him/herself.
